docs.title: External Dependencies
docs.text:
These are the software packages iProc was developed with and validated against.

Can I upgrade these dependencies?

iProc may run perfectly fine with newer versions of the dependencies listed below, but the results have never been validated. In other words, YMMV.

Software Versions Python 3.11 GNU Parallel 20180522 MRIcron 2012_12 AFNI 2016_09_04 FSL 4.0.3, 5.0.4, 5.0.10, 6.0.1 MRIcroGL 2019_09_04 FreeSurfer 6.0.0 ImageMagick 6.7.8-10 ANTs 2.4.4 dcm2niix 1.0.20230411

docs.title: FSL, FSL, Everywhere
docs.text:
Important

Until there is a portable solution in place (e.g., containers), end users need to modify the following lines of code to load the correct version of FSL at the appropriate time.

iProc dynamically switches the FSL version at runtime using Lmod. Your environment and/or module names may differ.

docs.title: iProc.py
docs.text: Before invoking iProc.py, it is assumed that you have already loaded FSL v5.0.10.

docs.title: fmap_from_bids
docs.text:
Within runscript/fmap_from_bids.py, the FSL version is switched to v4.0.3 before running the FSL bet2 command.

cmd = f'module load fsl/4.0.3-ncf && {_cmd}'

docs.title: fmap_topup_prep
docs.text:
Within runscript/fmap_topup_prep.sh, the FSL version is switched to v6.0.1 before running the FSL topup command

module load fsl/6.0.1-ncf

docs.title: fm_unwarp_and_mc_to_midvol
docs.text:
In runscript/fm_unwarp_and_mc_to_midvol.sh, the FSL version is switched to v4.0.3 before invoking runscript/fm_uw.sh, then switched back to v5.0.4

${CODEDIR}/modwrap.sh 'module load fsl/4.0.3-ncf' 'module load fsl/5.0.4-ncf' [...]

docs.title: fmap_fsl_prepare_fieldmap_prep
docs.text:
In runscript/fmap_fsl_prepare_fieldmap_prep.sh, the FSL version is switched to v5.0.4 before invoking the FSL fsl_prepare_fieldmap command

module load fsl/5.0.4-ncf

docs.title: Welcome to the iProc Quickstart
docs.text: Before you can proceed with the next mind-bending scientific discovery, you need to confirm that you have a fully functioning iProc installation. This guide will help you.

docs.title: clone the repository
docs.text:
First, you need to download iProc by cloning the repository from GitHub

git clone -b v2.6.0-beta.2 https://github.com/harvard-nrg/iProc

docs.title: create a virtual environment
docs.text:
iProc is primarily written in Python (and some bash scripts). You will need to create a Python virtual environment before you can move forward.

Python version

One of the iProc dependencies is numpy == 1.25.2, which is compatible with Python versions up to Python 3.11. If you want to use a newer version of Python, you will need to change pyproject.toml to install numpy == 2.0.0 or greater. Be advised that upgrading dependencies has not been validated.

cd iProc
python3.11 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install --upgrade pip

docs.title: install iProc
docs.text:
To install iProc and its corresponding Python dependencies, run the following command within your activated virtual environment

pip install .

docs.title: install external dependencies
docs.text:
iProc requires several external software packages described on the following page.

FSL

iProc switches FSL several times at runtime. It is strongly advised that you read the following page to understand where and how this is done. You will almost certainly need to make modifications to iProc before it will run successfully within your environment.

docs.title: verify your installation
docs.text:
Run the following command to verify that you are able to launch iProc

./iProc.py --help

If you're here, you are well on your way to getting iProc up and running. Way to go

docs.title: Example dataset
docs.text:
We've created an example dataset for you to verfiy that you have a fully functioning version of iProc installed.

The following steps are choreographed and assume that you are running each command within your iProc base directory.

docs.title: download and extract the dataset archive
Run iProc
docs.text: Now for the part you've been waiting for. Let's run iProc. You got this

docs.title: set the base directory
docs.text:
You need to replace the {{BASEDIR}} placeholder within ASDF.cfg (line 3) with the location to your tutorial_data directory

DIR=$(pwd)/tutorial_data
sed -ir s@{{BASEDIR}}@${DIR}@g tutorial_data/mri_data/ASDF/subject_lists/ASDF.cfg

docs.title: set the fsaverage6 directory
docs.text:
You also need to replace the {{FSDIR}} placeholder within ASDF.cfg (last line) with the location to the fsaverage6 directory under your FreeSurfer installation

DIR=${FREESURFER_HOME}/fsaverage6
sed -ir s@{{FSDIR}}@${DIR}@g tutorial_data/mri_data/ASDF/subject_lists/ASDF.cfg

docs.title: run iProc
docs.text:
The time has come. Run the following command to launch the iProc setup stage

./iProc.py --config tutorial_data/mri_data/subject_lists/ASDF.cfg --executor local --stage setup --bids $(pwd)/bids/sub-ASDF

After each stage, iProc will guide you to the next stage.
